does: This paper empirically evaluates the reliability of large language models used as judges for comparing AI-generated outputs. The authors conduct repeated identical evaluations across 29 tasks using GPT-4o-mini and GPT-4.1-mini, performing 50 pairwise and 50 pointwise comparison trials per question. They measure preference flips—instances where the judge reverses its ranking of the same pair across runs—and find that preferences flip on average 13.6% of the time, with 28% of questions exceeding 20% flip rates and some reaching 56%. They also uncover systematic biases, including first-position bias in GPT-4o-mini (72% A-majority), and a significant gap between pairwise and pointwise judgments: judges declare winners even when their own 10-point scalar scores show only small differences of 0.19–0.36 points. Temperature and prompt-sensitivity ablations further characterize sources of instability.
connects: This work builds on growing concerns about LLM-based evaluation methods, which have become standard practice for ranking model outputs, training reward models, and populating leaderboards despite limited validation. It extends prior work questioning evaluation reliability by providing the first large-scale systematic study of run-to-run instability in LLM judges. The paper relates to broader literature on benchmark reliability and human-AI alignment, as well as research on prompt sensitivity and temperature effects in language models. It responds to the widespread adoption of LLM-as-a-Judge without rigorous characterization of its consistency, echoing earlier concerns about automated evaluation metrics and the importance of reproducibility in AI evaluation pipelines.
enables: This work opens several important directions for improving AI evaluation practices. It motivates development of more reliable and calibrated judge models, such as models specifically trained for consistency or uncertainty-aware judges that express confidence levels. The findings enable better practices for leaderboard design, such as requiring multiple evaluation runs, using ensemble judges, or implementing consensus mechanisms to reduce single-judge noise. It points toward standardized reliability benchmarks for evaluation methods themselves, similar to how we validate scientific instruments. The work also enables research into understanding why LLMs exhibit these judgment inconsistencies—whether from calibration, tokenization effects, or fundamental uncertainty—and could motivate alternatives like fine-tuned or ensemble approaches. Finally, it supports future work on developing evaluation methods with provable consistency guarantees or robustness to prompt and temperature variation.
